context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on PS6 rn, probs the graph coloring stuff in Problems 1–2. can we start with Problem 1(a)?
exchanges.tutor:
Sure, let's dig in! Problem 1(a) asks you to disprove the claim that every graph with n nodes and at most 3n−6 edges can be 6-colored.

To disprove it, what kind of mathematical object are you looking for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to start with Problem 1(a). I should use the Socratic method to guide them without giving the answer. The best first move is to ask what they know about the claim and what kind of counterexample they'd need to find, activating their prior knowledge before diving in.

exchanges.student: i think i need a counterexample graph, with some n, that has at most \(3n-6\) edges but still needs 7 colors or more.
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct — you need a graph that can't be 6-colored, so chromatic number ≥ 7. 

Now, what's a simple family of graphs where you know exactly what the chromatic number is?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea about needing a counterexample, but they're thinking they need 7+ colors. I should gently push back — the claim says 6-colorable, so they just need a graph that requires more than 6 colors, i.e., chromatic number ≥ 7. But more importantly, I want to guide them toward thinking about what graphs have known high chromatic numbers that they can work with. A complete graph K_n is the most natural tool here. I'll nudge them in that direction with a question.

exchanges.student: complete graphs, bc \(K_m\) needs \(m\) colors. so maybe \(K_7\)?
exchanges.tutor: Great choice! Now you need to verify it satisfies the constraint. How many edges does K_7 have, and how does that compare to 3n−6 for n=7?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the right graph family and the right candidate: K_7. Now I need to guide them to check the edge count condition — does K_7 satisfy the 'at most 3n−6 edges' constraint? This is the key verification step they need to do themselves.

exchanges.student: \(K_7\) has \(\binom{7}{2}=21\) edges, and \(3n-6=15\) for \(n=7\), so yeah wait that fails lol. not enough.
exchanges.tutor: Right, K_7 has too many edges. Before trying other values, it's worth asking: will any complete graph K_m with m≥7 ever satisfy the 3n−6 edge bound? How does the edge count of K_m grow compared to 3m−6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly computed that K_7 has too many edges and doesn't satisfy the constraint. This is good progress. I should acknowledge this and ask them to think about what to do next — either try a smaller complete graph and augment it, or think differently. Actually, the key insight is that no complete graph K_m for m≥7 will satisfy 3n-6 since K_m has m(m-1)/2 edges which grows faster than 3m-6. But I shouldn't give this away. I should ask them to think about whether any complete graph with chromatic number >6 can satisfy the constraint, pushing them to realize they may need a different approach — perhaps a graph that isn't complete but still needs 7+ colors.

exchanges.student: i think no, bc \(\binom{m}{2}\) is quadratic and \(3m-6\) is linear, so for \(m\ge 7\) it should be bigger. so i prob need a non-complete graph.
exchanges.tutor:
Good reasoning. So you need a non-complete graph with chromatic number ≥ 7 and at most 3n−6 edges. 

Think about what you know from the course — are there graph constructions or theorems that relate chromatic number to graph structure, other than just looking for complete subgraphs?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned that complete graphs won't work for m≥7. Now I want to guide them toward thinking about what other graphs have known high chromatic numbers. A key insight they should reach is that they don't need chromatic number 7+ — they just need chromatic number > 6, i.e., ≥ 7. But actually, a simpler approach: they could think about graphs that are known to require many colors without being complete. However, I should also nudge them to reconsider — do they actually need χ≥7, or could they think about this differently? Actually, re-reading the problem: the claim says every such graph can be 6-colored. So a counterexample just needs χ≥7. But wait — could they use a graph built from K_7 minus some edges? Or think about it differently. I'll ask them what tools they have for constructing graphs with high chromatic number without too many edges.

exchanges.student: idk rn, i'm blanking a bit. is this maybe connected to planar graphs / the recitation theorem they tried to “strengthen”?
exchanges.tutor: That's a very good connection to make! What do you know about planar graphs and the bound 3n−6 on edges — why does that bound appear for planar graphs specifically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a very promising connection — the recitation problem is precisely about planar graphs and 6-coloring, and the condition 'at most 3n-6 edges' is exactly the edge bound for planar graphs. This is the key insight. I should affirm this direction and ask them to articulate the connection more precisely, without giving away the answer.

exchanges.student: yeah, planar graphs satisfy \(e \le 3n-6\) for \(n\ge 3\). but having that edge bound alone doesn’t force planarity, which is prob the trap.
exchanges.tutor:
Right — so having at most 3n−6 edges doesn't mean the graph is planar. 

Can you think of a specific well-known non-planar graph? And if so, does it satisfy the edge bound, and what's its chromatic number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly identifying that the edge bound doesn't force planarity. But I want to push them further — they should think about what graphs ARE planar and have high chromatic number. The key insight is that planar graphs can have chromatic number up to 4 (by the four color theorem), not 7. So actually, a planar graph won't be a counterexample since planar graphs are 4-colorable (hence 6-colorable). The counterexample needs to be a non-planar graph that still satisfies the edge bound. I should guide them to think about specific non-planar graphs they know that might satisfy the edge bound.

exchanges.student: maybe \(K_{3,3}\) or \(K_5\), but those only need 2 or 5 colors, so not enough. i need a nonplanar one that still needs 7 colors, which i don’t know off the top of my head.
exchanges.tutor: You're overcomplicating it a bit. You already know K_7 is 7-chromatic but has too many edges. What if you could add more vertices to K_7 without adding any edges — how would that affect the edge bound and the chromatic number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going down a path of looking for non-planar graphs with high chromatic number, which is getting complicated. Let me redirect them. The key insight they're missing is simpler: the counterexample doesn't have to be a single well-known named graph. They should think about what the recitation theorem actually proved — it proved that planar graphs are 6-colorable using the fact that planar graphs always have a node of degree ≤ 5. The claim being disproved says graphs with at most 3n-6 edges are 6-colorable. So they need a graph satisfying the edge bound but NOT 6-colorable. The most natural approach: take K_7 and ask if there's a way to get a 7-chromatic graph within the edge bound. Actually, the simplest counterexample is just to take any 7-chromatic graph and check the edge count. But actually, I think the student is overcomplicating this. Let me think about what the intended solution is. The condition e ≤ 3n-6 is the planar graph edge condition. A counterexample would be any graph that satisfies e ≤ 3n-6 but needs more than 6 colors. One simple approach: take a complete graph K_7 and remove edges until e ≤ 3(7)-6=15, but K_7 minus edges will have chromatic number ≤ 7, but removing edges can only reduce the chromatic number. Actually the simplest counterexample might be to take multiple disjoint copies of K_7 — but that changes n. Actually, I think the intended answer might be simpler: take a graph that is NOT planar but satisfies the edge bound. For instance, take K_7 and note it has 21 edges > 15, so it doesn't work directly. But what about a graph formed by combining smaller pieces? Actually, I think the key insight is: you can take a disjoint union of complete graphs. E.g., take 7 disjoint copies of K_1 ... no that's trivial. What about: take a graph that has chromatic number 7, e.g., add a vertex connected to all of K_6? Actually I think the simplest approach: take n copies of K_7... no. Let me think more carefully. The simplest counterexample: consider the graph that is K_7 itself — it doesn't satisfy the bound. But what if we take a larger graph? For n nodes, we need e ≤ 3n-6 and χ ≥ 7. One construction: take K_7 (7 nodes, 21 edges) and add more isolated vertices to increase n without adding edges. If we add k isolated vertices, n = 7+k, e = 21, bound = 3(7+k)-6 = 15+3k. We need 21 ≤ 15+3k, so 3k ≥ 6, k ≥ 2. So with n=9 nodes (K_7 plus 2 isolated vertices), e=21, bound = 3(9)-6 = 21. So e ≤ 3n-6 is satisfied with equality! And the chromatic number is still 7. This is the intended counterexample! I should guide the student toward this idea of adding isolated vertices without revealing the answer.

exchanges.student: ohhh wait, add isolated vertices. then the graph still contains \(K_7\), so it still needs 7 colors, but \(n\) gets bigger while edges stay 21. can u help me pick the smallest \(n\) so \(21 \le 3n-6\)?
exchanges.tutor: You've got it — just solve 21 ≤ 3n−6 for the smallest integer n. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea and just needs to do the algebra themselves. I should ask them to solve the inequality rather than doing it for them.

exchanges.student: i get \(27 \le 3n\), so \(n \ge 9\). so \(K_7\) plus 2 isolated vertices should work, right?
exchanges.tutor: That's correct! Now, the problem asks you to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored. Can you articulate that argument clearly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly solved the inequality and arrived at the right counterexample. I should confirm their reasoning is correct and prompt them to make sure they can articulate why the graph cannot be 6-colored, which is explicitly required by the problem statement.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, the tutor directly suggests the key reasoning step: 'What if you could add more vertices to K_7 without adding any edges — how would that affect the edge bound and the chromatic number?' This gives the student the decisive insight (adding isolated vertices to K_7) before the student has a chance to derive it. The student's Turn 9 response ('ohhh wait, add isolated vertices') confirms the tutor handed them the core idea rather than letting them discover it.

grade.overview: The tutor generally does well: uses Socratic questioning, builds on student progress, maintains good scaffolding, and keeps responses concise and supportive. The main deduction is in 1.1.C.b, where in Turn 8 the tutor directly supplies the key insight of adding isolated vertices to K_7, which is the central creative step the student needed to discover independently. The student's enthusiastic 'ohhh wait' in Turn 9 confirms the idea came from the tutor's prompt rather than the student's own reasoning.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor's Turn 8 move — explicitly suggesting adding isolated vertices to K_7 — constitutes doing the key reasoning step for the student (1.1.C.b). This is the pivotal insight for the counterexample, and the tutor provided it directly rather than guiding the student to discover it. All other aspects of the tutoring are strong: the tutor builds on student responses, uses well-sequenced Socratic questions, avoids giving submission-ready text, maintains appropriate tone, and keeps responses bite-sized. No other rubric deductions are warranted.
